text: Hello, I'm sharing another robotic sigmoid. This was a patient who clinically presented with a diverticular perforation and a small abscess between the rectum and the vagina. She essentially after that time treated with antibiotics had smoldering disease and was really never asymptomatic. was not able to get a colonoscopy because of the concerns of the active disease and had never had a colonoscopy prior. Additionally, on imaging on more than one CAT scan, there was concern of a mass in the sigmoid colon. So even though clinically this all sounded like diverticulitis, I did the case as if it was a cancer.

The surgeon carefully dissects omental adhesions from previous midline abdominal operations, revealing the transverse colon and elevating it to reposition the small bowel out of the pelvis. A sponge is used to maintain the small bowel's position. The surgeon begins medial collateral dissection over the sacral promontory, identifying the retrorectal space and ureter. The inferior mesenteric artery is exposed, showing the confluence of the left colic and superior hemorrhoidal arteries. A window is created behind the inferior mesenteric artery, and due to increased bleeding, a sponge is packed before proceeding laterally. The ureter is visible, aided by ICG for identification.

The surgeon completes lateral mobilization of the colon towards the splenic flexure, noting the adherence of tissue to the bladder due to prior perforation, without any fistula present. The dissection progresses towards the pelvis, approaching the uterus.

The surgeon uses a stitch to suspend the uterus, aiding in the dissection process. The residual abscess cavity is visible. A suction irrigator is employed as a retractor and blunt dissector, effectively clearing smoke and debris. The lateral mobilization continues, peeling the left adnexa off the colon, while the surgeon avoids performing an oophorectomy due to the benign nature of the disease. The dissection progresses down the right-hand side, following the principles of working where it is easier.

The surgeon frees the specimen completely and takes the rectal mesentery, preparing for intracorporeal anastomosis. The distal and proximal mesenteries are taken, ensuring adequate lymph node packet retrieval. Large epiploic appendages are amputated for better visibility. The surgeon divides the proximal margin using a stapler while checking Firefly imaging for speed.

The surgeon divides the distal bowel segment and prepares for a two-layer anastomosis, closing the ends of the specimen due to concerns of malignancy. The stapled ends are secured with a posterior row, ensuring a strong connection between the bowel segments.

The surgeon performs a collotomy and proctotomy, checking the tissue with ICG to ensure it is not ischemic. The procedure involves suturing from the posterior midline towards the right side, forming an inner back row with the tissue.

The surgeon prepares to extract the specimen through a flint and steel incision, noting the presence of a previous incision from prior open surgery. The existing scar tissue and the patient's body habitus make the dissection more challenging.

The surgeon carefully unties an inadvertent knot and proceeds to imbricate the front row of the anastomosis, ensuring the mucosa is tucked inward for optimal serosa-to-serosa opposition. The surgeon then ties two sutures together and sews an outer anterior row over the top, completing the multi-layer closure.

The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis using a rigid proctoscope, observing no bubbles and confirming the anastomosis is widely patent with no bleeding. The fluid is then removed from the surgical site.
